sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 2-week history of right shoulder pain, initially exacerbated by work projects in the backyard, including lifting and physical activity.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is more tender to the touch when raising the arm.
The patient reports no restriction in arm movement, but experiences pain with movement and at night when sleeping on the affected side.
The patient has taken Advil and applied ice to alleviate symptoms, with some temporary relief.
The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain, particularly on the right side, dating back to a childhood injury from playing baseball.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain, particularly on the right side, dating back to a childhood injury from playing baseball.
Review of systems: The patient reports no numbness in the arm or hands.
Current medications: The patient is taking Advil for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are: temperature 97.8°F, heart rate 62, respirations 16, blood pressure 116/84, and oxygen saturation 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip, and on neurovascular exam of the right arm, capillary refill is less than 3 seconds and sensation to touch is intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder x-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, which is an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; Active; Suspected; Past shoulder pain; Active; Suspected.
Plan: I prescribed Advil as needed for pain relief.
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the patient's shoulder.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks.
I advised the patient to continue taking Tylenol and consider a steroid injection if symptoms do not improve.
I recommended soaking the patient's feet in Epsom salts to alleviate foot and ankle soreness.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in approximately 6-8 weeks to reassess symptoms and progress with physical therapy.
